title: Shift of the maxima of the critical currents of different polarity relative to the zero magnetic flux along the flux axis in a superconducting asymmetric aluminum ring
authors: V. I. Kuznetsov, O. V. Trofimov
abstract: We measured the rectification of an ac voltage in a structure of superconducting circularly-asymmetric aluminum rings in series, permeated with a magnetic flux and biased with a low-frequency alternating current (without a dc component). This rectification is due to the shift of the maxima of the critical currents of different polarity relative to the zero flux in opposite directions along the flux axis in the asymmetric ring. For the first time, we propose a model for a temperature-dependent phase shift equal to difference between dimensionless kinetic inductances of wide and narrow semirings having the same length and thickness. The shift is not zero in the case of different critical currents densities in both semirings. This is possible only in a situation of different critical temperatures of both semirings. The model describes well the temperature-dependent shift of the maxima of the critical currents, answers the long-standing mysterious challenge of the shift and removes extremely strange contradiction between the results of different measurements, previously found in circularly-asymmetric aluminum structures.
summary: This paper resolves a long-standing mystery regarding the phase shift in asymmetric superconducting rings by attributing it to differences in kinetic inductance between the ring's arms. The proposed model successfully explains the temperature dependence of this shift and reconciles previous experimental contradictions. This provides a clearer understanding of how geometric asymmetry and local critical temperature variations affect superconducting transport.
main_problem: Explaining the mysterious temperature-dependent phase shift of critical current maxima of different polarities relative to zero magnetic flux in asymmetric superconducting aluminum rings.
main_result: The authors propose a model where the phase shift is caused by the difference in dimensionless kinetic inductances between the wide and narrow semirings, driven by different critical temperatures in each arm.
method: The study combines experimental measurements of rectified AC voltage and resistance oscillations in series-connected aluminum rings with a theoretical model based on Ginzburg-Landau theory.
model_or_system: The system consists of circularly-asymmetric aluminum rings (wide and narrow semirings) fabricated via electron beam lithography. The model treats the asymmetric ring as an asymmetric dc micro-SQUID where the phase shift is linked to kinetic inductance differences.
key_observables: Rectified voltage (V_rec), critical currents of different polarities (Ic+ and Ic-), resistance oscillations (dR/dPhi), and AC voltage oscillograms (Vac).
important_parameters: Temperature near Tc (approx 1.31 K), normalized magnetic flux (Phi/Phi0), kinetic inductance (Lk), and critical temperatures of the semirings (Tcw and Tcn).
main_assumptions: The magnetic inductance is negligible compared to kinetic inductance; the current-phase relationship is nearly sinusoidal; and the critical current densities are independent of the magnetic field within the experimental range.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows circulating current and current jumps; Figure 5 displays AC voltage waveforms at specific flux points; Figure 6 and 7 present experimental phase shifts and theoretical fits for various single and multi-ring structures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of the phase shift, describes the experimental setup and fabrication of asymmetric rings, presents the proposed kinetic inductance model, details the temperature-dependent measurements, and concludes by validating the model against experimental data across multiple ring configurations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (Color online) Resistive N-S transition Rr3(T) measured on a struc- ture of three circularly-asymmetric rings in series at Idc = 0.1 µA. Inset upper: expected normalized circulating current Ir/Irm as a function of Φ/Φ0 in a su- perconducting circularly-symmetric ring of small radius at T close to Tc. Inset below: a sketch of one of the rings included in the structure of three rings in series.

Figure 2: (Color online) Rectiﬁed direct voltage Vrec1(Φ/Φ0) as a function of Φ/Φ0 measured in two rings in series biased with an alternating cosine current (without a dc component) Iac1(t) with the frequency ν = 1.5333 kHz and the ac amplitude Iacm1 = 3.88 µA greater than the critical current Ic(T, B = 0) at T = 1.293 K. The numbers 1, 2, and 3 are placed near the marked points of the Vrec1(Φ/Φ0) curve corresponding to three values Φ/Φ0 = 0, 1-0.16, and 2, respectively. At these points, the oscillograms of the ac voltage Vac1(Φ/Φ0, t) were taken at the same ac Iac1(t).

Figure 4: (Color online) Lines 1, 2, and 3 are oscillograms of the ac voltage Vac1(Φ/Φ0, t), taken on two rings in series at Φ/Φ0 = 0, 1-0.16, and 2, corre- sponding to the marked points 1, 2 and 3 (curve Vrec1(Φ/Φ0), Fig. 2), respec- tively. Lines 1 and 3 are the same. Line 4 is the oscillogram of the applied ac Iac1(t) (the coordinate axis of the current is located on the right). Line 5 is the ac voltage Vac1(t) = Iac1(t)Rr2.

Figure 3: (Color online) Rectiﬁed voltage Vrec2(Φ/Φ0) as a function of Φ/Φ0, recorded on a structure of three rings in series biased with an alternating cosine current (without a dc component) Iac2(t) with the frequency ν = 1.5333 kHz and the ac amplitude Iacm2 = 5.5 µA less than Ic(T, B = 0) at T = 1.288 K. The numbers 1, 2, and 3 are placed near the marked points of the Vrec2 curve corresponding to three values Φ/Φ0 = 5−0.16, 7+0.5, and 15+0.5, respectively. At these points, the voltage oscillograms Vac2(Φ/Φ0, t) are measured at the same ac Iac2(t).

Figure 5: (Color online) Lines 1 (solid), 2 (dash-dotted), and 3 (dotted) are oscillograms of the ac voltage Vac2(Φ/Φ0, t) recorded on a structure of three rings in series at Φ/Φ0 = 5 −0.16, 7+0.5, and 15+0.5, corresponding to the marked points 1, 2 and 3 (curve Vrec2(Φ/Φ0), Fig. 3), respectively. Line 4 is the oscillogram of the applied ac Iac2(t) (the coordinate axis of the current is on the right). Line 5 is the ac voltage Vac2(t) = Iac2(t)Rr3.
